tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj tekst. Do każdej luki (1–6) dopasuj właściwe zdanie (A–H), aby powstał logiczny i spójny tekst. Dwa zdania zostały podane dodatkowo i nie pasują do żadnej luki.
tasks.text:
HOW BEES MAKE HONEY

Honey is one of the most remarkable foods produced in the natural world. It is made by honeybees from the nectar of flowering plants, and the process by which they transform a thin, sugary liquid into thick, golden honey is both complex and fascinating. 1. _________ A single hive may contain up to 60,000 bees, all working together with extraordinary efficiency.

The process begins when forager bees fly out from the hive in search of flowers. Using a combination of eyesight and their sensitivity to ultraviolet light, they locate blooms that are rich in nectar. 2. _________ The nectar mixes with enzymes in the bee's body, beginning the chemical transformation into honey even before the bee returns to the hive.

Back at the hive, the forager passes the nectar to younger worker bees through a process called trophallaxis, where the liquid is passed from mouth to mouth. 3. _________ Eventually, the processed nectar is deposited into a wax cell in the honeycomb.

At this stage, the nectar still contains too much water to be stored safely as honey. 4. _________ This creates a warm airflow through the hive that rapidly evaporates the excess moisture. When the water content drops to around 17 percent, the honey is ready.

The bees then seal each cell with a thin layer of beeswax to preserve the honey. 5. _________ Its low water content, high sugar concentration, and natural acidity all combine to prevent the growth of bacteria and mould.

Humans have been harvesting honey for thousands of years. Cave paintings in Spain dating back 8,000 years show figures climbing to reach wild beehives. 6. _________ Today, honey is still valued not only as a food but also for its medicinal properties, which include helping to soothe sore throats and heal minor wounds.

options.text: They drink the nectar and store it in a special second stomach known as the honey stomach.
Each exchange adds more enzymes and reduces the water content of the liquid.
To drive out moisture, thousands of bees fan their wings over the open cells simultaneously.
This is what makes honey one of the very few foods that never spoils, even after thousands of years.
The ancient Egyptians kept bees domestically and used honey both as a sweetener and as an offering to the gods.
Honey production is a highly organised collective effort that depends on every bee performing its specific role.
Bees also produce beeswax, royal jelly, and propolis, all of which have uses for both the hive and for humans.
The queen bee is responsible for laying all the eggs in the hive and can produce up to 2,000 a day.
